main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Honey: a synergistic progressive training approach for federated learning that integrates holistic view and block-wise feedback","Elastic resource harmonization protocol allowing devices to adaptively train specific layers based on memory capacity"],"problems":["Memory limitation in federated learning on mobile/IoT devices","Accuracy degradation in progressive training due to isolated block training and lack of collaboration between blocks","Inefficient resource utilization when devices are confined to training only designated blocks"],"datasets":["CIFAR10","CIFAR100","SVHN","STL-10","Tiny-ImageNet"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Peak memory usage"],"results":[{"metric":"Average accuracy improvement","value":"up to 43.9% over state-of-the-art approaches"},{"metric":"Peak memory usage reduction","value":"up to 49%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a synergistic progressive training approach (Honey) that fuses holistic view and block-wise feedback to align block training with the global objective and enhance block collaboration.","Introduces an elastic resource harmonization protocol that allows devices to dynamically choose the number of layers to train based on their memory capacity, optimizing resource utilization and breaking gradient isolation between blocks.","Demonstrates significant accuracy improvements and memory savings over existing memory-efficient and progressive training baselines."]}

methods: Honey: a synergistic progressive training approach for federated learning that integrates holistic view and block-wise feedback
Elastic resource harmonization protocol allowing devices to adaptively train specific layers based on memory capacity
problems: Memory limitation in federated learning on mobile/IoT devices
Accuracy degradation in progressive training due to isolated block training and lack of collaboration between blocks
Inefficient resource utilization when devices are confined to training only designated blocks
novelty_claims: Proposes a synergistic progressive training approach (Honey) that fuses holistic view and block-wise feedback to align block training with the global objective and enhance block collaboration.

Introduces an elastic resource harmonization protocol that allows devices to dynamically choose the number of layers to train based on their memory capacity, optimizing resource utilization and breaking gradient isolation between blocks.
Demonstrates significant accuracy improvements and memory savings over existing memory-efficient and progressive training baselines.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ProFL: a progressive federated learning framework that divides the model into blocks and trains them sequentially (progressive training)","Progressive model shrinking: training and freezing front blocks with designed output modules to assist feature learning and parameter initialization","Progressive model growing: utilizing output modules to grow the model progressively after shrinking","A novel scalar metric to assess the learning status of each block and determine when to trigger the next block's training"],"problems":["High memory footprint of federated learning models prevents deployment on memory-constrained heterogeneous devices","Difficulty in training large models on devices with limited memory resources"],"datasets":["Representative models and datasets (exact names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Peak memory footprint","Model accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Peak memory footprint reduction","value":"up to 57.4%"},{"metric":"Model accuracy improvement","value":"up to 82.4%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposing ProFL, a progressive FL framework that breaks the memory wall for heterogeneous devices","Introducing a block-wise progressive training approach to reduce memory footprint during FL","Designing output modules for each block to preserve feature representation and assist in parameter initialization","Proposing a novel scalar metric to control the training pace of each block","Theoretical proof of convergence for the proposed method"]}

methods: ProFL: a progressive federated learning framework that divides the model into blocks and trains them sequentially (progressive training)
Progressive model shrinking: training and freezing front blocks with designed output modules to assist feature learning and parameter initialization
Progressive model growing: utilizing output modules to grow the model progressively after shrinking
A novel scalar metric to assess the learning status of each block and determine when to trigger the next block's training
problems: High memory footprint of federated learning models prevents deployment on memory-constrained heterogeneous devices
Difficulty in training large models on devices with limited memory resources
datasets: Representative models and datasets (exact names not specified in the provided content)
novelty_claims: Proposing ProFL, a progressive FL framework that breaks the memory wall for heterogeneous devices
Introducing a block-wise progressive training approach to reduce memory footprint during FL
Designing output modules for each block to preserve feature representation and assist in parameter initialization
Proposing a novel scalar metric to control the training pace of each block
Theoretical proof of convergence for the proposed method

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ProFL: a progressive training framework for federated learning that partitions the model into blocks and trains each block sequentially, freezing blocks after convergence","Progressive model shrinking: constructing output modules and acquiring initialization parameters for each block by training blocks from back to front","Progressive model growing: sequentially training each block from front to back using the output modules and initialization parameters","Block freezing determination module: assesses block convergence and freezes blocks upon convergence","Novel scalar-based metric (effective movement): measures the movement distance of scalars in a block to determine convergence"],"problems":["High memory requirements of model training in federated learning prevent deployment on resource-constrained devices","Existing solutions either require public datasets (which are often unavailable) or compromise model architecture/performance","Need for a federated learning approach that allows heterogeneous devices with limited memory to participate without sacrificing model capacity or requiring public data"],"datasets":["ImageNet"],"metrics":["Peak memory footprint","Model accuracy","Effective movement (novel scalar-based metric for block convergence)"],"results":[{"metric":"Peak memory footprint reduction","value":"up to 57.4%"},{"metric":"Model accuracy improvement","value":"up to 82.4%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ProFL, a new progressive training paradigm for federated learning that enables memory-constrained devices to participate by training model blocks sequentially and freezing them after convergence","Introduces a two-stage training process (progressive model shrinking and growing) to preserve feature representation and optimize block initialization","Presents a novel scalar-based metric (effective movement) to accurately determine block convergence and enable safe freezing","Theoretically proves convergence of the proposed method and demonstrates significant memory savings and accuracy improvements in experiments"]}

methods: ProFL: a progressive training framework for federated learning that partitions the model into blocks and trains each block sequentially, freezing blocks after convergence
Progressive model shrinking: constructing output modules and acquiring initialization parameters for each block by training blocks from back to front
Progressive model growing: sequentially training each block from front to back using the output modules and initialization parameters
Block freezing determination module: assesses block convergence and freezes blocks upon convergence
Novel scalar-based metric (effective movement): measures the movement distance of scalars in a block to determine convergence
problems: High memory requirements of model training in federated learning prevent deployment on resource-constrained devices
Existing solutions either require public datasets (which are often unavailable) or compromise model architecture/performance
Need for a federated learning approach that allows heterogeneous devices with limited memory to participate without sacrificing model capacity or requiring public data
metrics: Effective movement (novel scalar-based metric for block convergence)
novelty_claims: Proposes ProFL, a new progressive training paradigm for federated learning that enables memory-constrained devices to participate by training model blocks sequentially and freezing them after convergence
Introduces a two-stage training process (progressive model shrinking and growing) to preserve feature representation and optimize block initialization
Presents a novel scalar-based metric (effective movement) to accurately determine block convergence and enable safe freezing
Theoretically proves convergence of the proposed method and demonstrates significant memory savings and accuracy improvements in experiments

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["NeuLite framework","Elastic progressive training","Model division into blocks with progressive training","Curriculum Mentor (curriculum-aware training losses for each block)","Training Harmonizer (parameter co-adaptation training paradigm, output modules for each block)"],"problems":["High memory footprint in federated learning on resource-constrained devices","Inefficiency of traditional FL methods that update the full model throughout training","Information isolation across model blocks during progressive training"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Peak memory usage","Model performance (accuracy or similar metric)","Training speed (acceleration factor)"],"results":[{"metric":"Peak memory usage reduction","value":"up to 50.4%"},{"metric":"Model performance improvement","value":"up to 84.2%"},{"metric":"Training acceleration","value":"up to 1.9X"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes NeuLite, a memory-efficient FL framework using elastic progressive training.","Introduces a curriculum mentor to provide curriculum-aware training losses for each model block.","Develops a training harmonizer for parameter co-adaptation and output modules to break information isolation across blocks.","Demonstrates significant memory savings, performance improvements, and training acceleration over traditional FL approaches."]}

methods: NeuLite framework

Model division into blocks with progressive training
Curriculum Mentor (curriculum-aware training losses for each block)
Training Harmonizer (parameter co-adaptation training paradigm, output modules for each block)
problems: High memory footprint in federated learning on resource-constrained devices

novelty_claims: Proposes NeuLite, a memory-efficient FL framework using elastic progressive training.

Develops a training harmonizer for parameter co-adaptation and output modules to break information isolation across blocks.
Demonstrates significant memory savings, performance improvements, and training acceleration over traditional FL approaches.

methods: FedStitch: a hierarchical coordination framework for federated learning using stitching of pre-trained blocks
Partitioning pre-trained models into blocks and composing new networks by stitching selected blocks
RL-weighted aggregator for block selection and aggregation in non-IID scenarios
Search space optimizer on the server to reduce candidate block pool size during stitching
Local energy optimizer (feedback-based frequency configuration) on clients to minimize energy consumption while maintaining training progress
problems: High memory footprint and energy consumption in federated learning, excluding low-end devices from participation
Degraded global model performance due to exclusion of resource-constrained devices
Inefficiency and high cost of training or fine-tuning large models on resource-limited devices
Handling data heterogeneity (non-IID data) in federated learning aggregation
Large search space for block selection from pre-trained models
Suboptimal energy efficiency during local block selection on edge devices
novelty_claims: First work to utilize pre-trained models without any training in federated learning (no back-propagation required)
Proposes a new neural network generation method by splitting and stitching blocks from multiple pre-trained models for downstream tasks in FL
Introduces an RL-based weighted aggregation algorithm with cross-validation to address non-IID data in FL
Presents an on-the-fly CKA-based search space reduction method for efficient block selection
Proposes a feedback-based frequency configuration method for energy-efficient local block selection on clients
Enables participation of highly resource-constrained clients in FL with minimal data and no training overhead, while achieving comparable or better performance to state-of-the-art methods

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel distillation-based semi-supervised federated learning (DS-FL) framework that exchanges model outputs (logits) instead of parameters, making communication cost independent of model size.

novelty_claims: FedRolex introduces a rolling sub-model extraction scheme for model-heterogeneous FL, enabling even training of all global server model parameters.
FedRolex can train a global server model larger than the largest client model, unlike prior static or random sub-model extraction methods.

FedRolex is fully compatible with secure aggregation protocols, enhancing privacy in FL.
FedRolex reduces communication overhead by only transmitting the required sub-model to each client.

methods: ProgFed: a federated progressive learning framework that divides the model into overlapping partitions and introduces lightweight local supervision heads, gradually increasing model capacity during training
Progressive training in federated learning, where training starts with shallower sub-models and progressively grows to the full model
Compatibility and combination with classical compression techniques (e.g., sparsification, quantization) and federated optimizations (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam)
problems: High computation and communication costs in federated learning, especially for edge devices with limited resources and bandwidth
Inefficiency of existing compression and pruning techniques in leveraging learning dynamics to reduce resource demands during federated training

results.value: up to 50x at only 0.1% loss in utility

results.metric: Performance improvement over standard training (with advanced optimizations)

novelty_claims: ProgFed is the first federated progressive learning framework that reduces both computation and two-way communication costs while preserving model utility.
ProgFed leverages progressive learning dynamics in federated learning, which has not been systematically explored before for resource reduction.
ProgFed is compatible with and complementary to existing compression and optimization techniques, enabling a wide range of trade-offs and further reductions in communication costs.
ProgFed achieves significant resource savings (computation and communication) without sacrificing model performance, and sometimes even outperforms baselines.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["InclusiveFL: a client-inclusive federated learning method that assigns models of different sizes to clients based on their computing capabilities","Layer-wise heterogeneous aggregation: sharing shallow bottom model layers between large and small models to align feature spaces and retain pre-trained knowledge","Momentum knowledge distillation: transferring knowledge from large models on powerful clients to small models on weak clients by encouraging top encoder layers in small models to imitate those in large models"],"problems":["Federated learning over heterogeneous devices with varying computing power and memory","Inclusion of weak clients with limited resources in federated learning without sacrificing model accuracy or fairness","Effective knowledge transfer between models of different sizes in federated learning","Avoiding under-representation and data loss from weak clients in federated learning"],"datasets":["Real-world benchmark datasets (specific datasets not named in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposing InclusiveFL, which allows all clients, regardless of device capability, to participate in federated learning by assigning models of different sizes","Introducing a layer-wise heterogeneous aggregation method to share shallow bottom layers between models of different sizes, aligning feature spaces and retaining pre-trained knowledge","Proposing a momentum knowledge distillation method to effectively transfer knowledge from large models to small models in federated learning over heterogeneous devices"]}

methods: InclusiveFL: a client-inclusive federated learning method that assigns models of different sizes to clients based on their computing capabilities
Layer-wise heterogeneous aggregation: sharing shallow bottom model layers between large and small models to align feature spaces and retain pre-trained knowledge

Momentum knowledge distillation: transferring knowledge from large models on powerful clients to small models on weak clients by encouraging top encoder layers in small models to imitate those in large models
problems: Federated learning over heterogeneous devices with varying computing power and memory
Inclusion of weak clients with limited resources in federated learning without sacrificing model accuracy or fairness

Proposing a momentum knowledge distillation method to effectively transfer knowledge from large models to small models in federated learning over heterogeneous devices

methods: FedZKT: Zero-shot Knowledge Transfer framework for federated learning with heterogeneous on-device models
Zero-shot federated distillation approach without private on-device data
Adversarially trained generator on the server using ensemble of collected on-device models

problems: Enabling federated learning with heterogeneous on-device models, especially for resource-constrained devices
Removing the need for public datasets or pre-trained data generators for knowledge transfer in federated learning
Facilitating participation of devices with varying computational resources in federated learning
Achieving effective knowledge transfer without access to private on-device data

results.value: FedZKT achieves higher accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods (exact values not specified)
FedZKT demonstrates better generalization than state-of-the-art methods (exact values not specified)

novelty_claims: FedZKT is the first framework to enable zero-shot knowledge transfer in federated learning with heterogeneous on-device models in a data-free manner.
FedZKT allows devices to independently design their on-device models based on local resources, supporting extensive participation from resource-constrained devices.
The zero-shot federated distillation approach does not require any public dataset or pre-trained data generator, removing data-dependency concerns.
A new softmax l1 (SL) loss function is proposed to facilitate zero-shot federated knowledge distillation.
FedZKT assigns compute-intensive distillation tasks to the server, reducing device workload and enabling participation from devices with limited resources.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FedMD framework (Federated Model Distillation)","Knowledge distillation for model-agnostic communication","Transfer learning from public datasets"],"problems":["Federated learning with heterogeneous (independently designed) models","Enabling collaboration without sharing model architectures or private data","Improving model performance with limited private data","Addressing model heterogeneity in federated learning"],"datasets":["MNIST","FEMNIST","CIFAR10","CIFAR100"],"metrics":["Test accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Average test accuracy gain per model (with 10 participants)","value":"20% gain over non-collaborative training"},{"metric":"Test accuracy gap to centralized (pooled data) training","value":"Only a few percent lower than centralized training"}],"novelty_claims":["FedMD is the first federated learning framework that allows each participant to independently design their own model architecture.","The framework enables knowledge transfer between heterogeneous models without sharing data or model details, using knowledge distillation.","FedMD only requires black-box access to models, not their internal architectures.","The approach leverages both transfer learning and knowledge distillation to enable effective collaboration among heterogeneous models."]}

Knowledge distillation for model-agnostic communication
Transfer learning from public datasets
problems: Federated learning with heterogeneous (independently designed) models
Enabling collaboration without sharing model architectures or private data

results.metric: Average test accuracy gain per model (with 10 participants)
Test accuracy gap to centralized (pooled data) training

novelty_claims: FedMD is the first federated learning framework that allows each participant to independently design their own model architecture.
The framework enables knowledge transfer between heterogeneous models without sharing data or model details, using knowledge distillation.
FedMD only requires black-box access to models, not their internal architectures.
The approach leverages both transfer learning and knowledge distillation to enable effective collaboration among heterogeneous models.

novelty_claims: Fed-ET is the first ensemble transfer algorithm for federated learning using unlabeled data that enables training a large server model with smaller, heterogeneous client models for any classification task.
Fed-ET introduces a weighted consensus distillation approach with diversity regularization to effectively filter out expert models and improve generalization in the presence of data heterogeneity.
Fed-ET allows client model heterogeneity and does not require clients to access public data, imposing no additional overhead on clients.

DEF-A (an accelerated version of DEF)
Random Blockwise Gradient Sparsification (RBGS) as the gradient compressor
problems: Communication bottleneck in large-scale distributed deep learning

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel detached error feedback (DEF) method for distributed SGD with random block-wise sparsification, motivated by a new trade-off between gradient variance and second moment.
Introduces DEF-A, which accelerates generalization and provides improved generalization bounds.
Establishes, for the first time, the connection between communication-efficient distributed SGD and SGD with iterate averaging (SGD-IA).
Provides theoretical and empirical evidence that DEF(-A) significantly improves performance over existing RBGS-based methods.

methods: FedGCS: a generative client selection framework for federated learning
Encoder-evaluator-decoder architecture trained on selection-score pairs
Gradient-based optimization in a continuous representation space for client selection
Beam search for generating optimal client selection
Automatic data collection from classical client selection methods (heuristic-based and learning-based)
problems: Efficient client selection in federated learning under statistical and system heterogeneity

results.value: FedGCS outperforms traditional methods (exact value not specified)
FedGCS achieves lower latency compared to baselines (exact value not specified)
FedGCS achieves lower energy consumption compared to baselines (exact value not specified)

novelty_claims: First to formulate client selection in federated learning as a generative task using a continuous representation space.
FedGCS encodes knowledge from diverse classical algorithms into a unified continuous space, enabling comprehensive optimization of multiple objectives (performance, latency, energy).
FedGCS leverages gradient-based optimization in a continuous space for client selection, which is more efficient than heuristic or RL-based approaches in large discrete spaces.
FedGCS requires only automatically collected selection-score data, reducing manual intervention and increasing flexibility and generalizability.
